title: College Athlete Profile: Porter Hurteau talks racing at UNH and what fuels him on and off the course - St. Albans Messenger
content:
Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. BFA-Fairfax varsity baseball coach Mike Brown, who’s led the… Laura Laramee, the coach of the BFA-St. Albans girls’ varsit… Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, a sophomore at the University of New Hampshire, studying chemical engineering, who’s also a three-season runner, began his running career at BFA-St. Albans, where he honed his skills, earning a spot with the Division 1 Wildcats. Why did you choose to study and run for UNH? For me, it was about finding somewhere that felt like home outside of Vermont. I grew up in a small town, and while I looked at the University of Vermont, I wanted an experience outside of the state. They have a great engineering program and a brand-new engineering building. I knew it was where I wanted to be. Why did you choose to study chemical engineering? Originally, I was going to go for mechanical engineering. I like to know the small details, and with chemical engineering, you’re working with the cellular level. It’s still a broad degree, and I’m getting a lot of opportunities and meeting a lot of new people in the industry. I really just wanted to try something new. Are you a car enthusiast? Yes, I like cars. In my junior year, I was sick of AP classes and decided to take automotive at Northwest Career and Technical Center, and it was super fun. I love working with vehicles—even every day cars. They’re machines, and you can figure them out with math. I love changing my own oil, headlights, and spark plugs. I love getting my hands dirty–compared to sitting in AP classes and listening to a lecture. My senior year, I took a class called Special Topics in Electric Aviation because it was taught by BETA Technologies. They were actual people from BETA who worked with Vermont State University. They took us through the simulators and through the class. I knew planes were cool, as were cars, and that’s how I got into chemical engineering. What is your goal for the use of your degree? I’d like to work with a performance division of an automotive company. You always have performance packages you can get, and I’d like to work on fuel systems and on making cars faster and more efficient. What do you enjoy about working with cars? I like math; I’ve been told my whole life I’m a good writer, but math has always been challenging for me, and I have to put time into learning it. I have a great memory, but I have to work at math and problem-solving. I like to sit down with math, work my way through it, and get the right answer. You know if you did it right, you did it right. If you didn’t, try again. There’s a sense of accomplishment for me when I’m sitting at a whiteboard and get the correct answer. What have you enjoyed about running for UNH? Honestly, I really enjoy being an athlete. So many people go to college to be students, and that’s why I’m here, but having something on campus that lets me escape the education side is awesome. I came to school and had a group of friends through sports. We spend at least two hours a day together, and we eat and spend time together. I love running and have done it competitively for seven years. It clears my head at the end of the day to go out and work hard with friends. We’re pushing our minds and bodies to the limit, and it’s awesome. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. What are some memorable moments in cross country after two seasons? There have been a lot. My first race was at my home course at Hard’ack in St. Albans, VT, and that was pretty special. I got to go back to the course I’ve raced and trained on forever. In my first race, I had a breakthrough. I ran a great race—was fourth for my team and top 15. We were there a month later for another race. The day before the meet, I got off the bus and saw my high school teammate Calvin Storm’s mom walking the course. It was special to be back, and the people in the town are like family. Do you have memorable moments from your indoor track seasons? Last season, I redshirted for indoor track, so this was my first indoor season racing. On Saturday, Jan. 31, I was at Boston University. I finished the race and saw my former teammates, Jacob and Teddy Tremblay, cheering for me from the UVM section and telling me what I had just run. Lucas MacKenzie, another former teammate, was also there. What are you looking forward to for your outdoor track season? I’m looking forward to this outdoor season. The conference will be at the University of Massachusetts, Lowell, where my former teammate Ehtan Mashtare runs. I’m looking forward to racing with my old teammates again. Ethan Mashtare, Jacob, and Teddy Tremblay all run in that conference, and Teddy just got welcomed to the America East, since he’s racing at UVM for his freshman year. We won’t be on the same team, but it will feel like home because we’ve raced together for so long. I’m excited to prove myself and show others what all of the BFA-St. Albans grads can do. What does it mean to you to see so many BFA-St. Albans alumni are racing at a high level, and what does it say about the program? I feel a sense of pride. Coach Mike Mashtare built this legacy over the last 45 years he’s been coaching, and I think it’s a testament to his knowledge and willingness to work personally with his athletes. He created this environment for growth in a cross-country high school team. We trained as hard as we could and still had fun. It really shows he was dialed in his training, and he’s willing to work with you. The strength we’ve exhibited in the America East Conference demonstrates the coaches' knowledge at BFA. Even now, Coach Mashtare shows up at the conference meets and sees us all racing. He’ll comment on form, and we know we can trust him. He’s there on the sidelines, and he’ll always be there for us. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Do you ever think about the Vermont connection while racing at Elle (Purrier) St. Pierre’s college? There’s definitely this feeling that her presence is always somewhere in the back of my mind. She’s a Vermonter, an Olympian, and a record holder. We saw her for the Hall of Fame induction last year. She’s ever-present, but the team and the coaches are focused on working with the athletes who are here in the present. What does it mean to you to grow up in a running family? Since we were young, we viewed our parents as role models. They’ve always been outdoors. We were sugaring in the spring, building huts in the woods, or going for family runs together. My dad has coached us up through high school and is coaching my brothers, Toby and Sam. It’s been nice to have that support. The first time I ran more than five miles was with my mother. I got dropped. We laugh looking back on it now that I’m a collegiate athlete. She’s always been there making granola bars to fuel us. My younger brothers have been an intrinsic motivator for me. I’ve wanted to lead by example, so I’ve always worked hard to be the best. Now that I’ve graduated from high school, it’s all about them and their last year of high school. We share our success and push each other. I’m proud of how hard they’ve worked and how our family has created this environment for us to realize our potential—so we’ve been able to do what we enjoy at the highest level. Do you have any wise words for younger athletes? Find something you love and are willing to put the work into. It’s hard to work hard for something you don’t want. If you have goals and want to reach them, work hard toward them. Try different things to reach those goals. It won’t be easy, but you’ll be willing to work a lot harder to achieve it. title: UNH's Grit to Grow helps teens explore careers, build leadership skills - Seacoastonline.com
content: Teens are invited to explore career opportunities and build leadership skills at the Grit to Grow Summit in the Lakes Region this June. The two-day event, hosted by the University of New Hampshire Extension in partnership with the Governor’s Task Force on Bring Back the Trades, will take place June 22-23, according to a community announcement. The summit aims to bring together teens, educators, employers, and community leaders to support young people as they consider life after high school. It will feature hands-on workshops, real-world career exploration, and discussions about college, trades, careers, and personal growth. “The saying, ‘It’s not just what you know, but who you know,’ is very relevant for our teens looking to determine their next steps along their career and education pathways,” says Kate Korbas, the state 4-H leader and youth development specialist, according to the announcement. The event will begin at Lakes Region Community College in Laconia, where teens will participate in interactive workshops focused on self-discovery, leadership, financial literacy, entrepreneurship, and storytelling. Sessions will cover topics such as paying bills, telling personal stories, and entrepreneurship. The second day will take place at the Inn at Mill Falls and is open to both overnight and one-day participants. Parents and caregivers are encouraged to attend alongside teens. During the second day, teens will engage in small group career exploration sessions with professionals from various industries, including skilled trades, healthcare, manufacturing, engineering, agriculture, hospitality, and government. Space is limited, and early registration is encouraged. The deadline to register is May 1. Scholarships are available for those seeking financial assistance. For registration and additional information, visit extension.unh.edu. This story was created by Kimberly Green, KGreen@usatodayco.com, with the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (AI). title: Ski industry workers are worried about climate change, UNH research shows - New Hampshire Public Radio
content:
New research from the University of New Hampshire shows professionals in the state’s ski industry are particularly worried about climate change. In a survey given last summer, 85% of New Hampshire ski professionals said they were either “alarmed” or “concerned” about climate change. Those results are much higher than the national average: 54%. More than half of the survey respondents did not think their industry in New Hampshire was doing enough to address global warming. The researchers said their results may point to motivation among ski industry workers to implement more sustainable practices and push for policy that could help limit the future impacts of climate change. “My hope is that the skiing community in New Hampshire recognizes that many of us share similar concerns about climate change,” researcher Elizabeth Burakowski said in a press release. “And, that we can collectively take action to reduce fossil fueled greenhouse gas emissions, use and produce energy responsibly and efficiently, and strategize practices that protect our winters while sustaining our outdoor recreational economy.” Get daily top stories from NHPR's newsroom with The Rundown. 
                        Check out all of NHPR's newsletters here. New Hampshire’s ski industry generates roughly $278.8 million each ski season, and supports 10,000 seasonal and 6,000 annual jobs, according to the researchers. Ski hills have tried to adapt, investing in snowmaking systems and branching into warm-weather activities like ziplining and biking. This winter was cold and snowy. But over time the ski season is shrinking, as New England winters warm. A statewide climate assessment from 2021 projected New Hampshire could see up to 50% less snow by 2099. The state averages around 100 days of deep natural snowpack sufficient for skiing each year. But, the researchers said, by the end of the century that could shrink to 60 or even 30 days, depending on future fossil fuel emissions. NHPR is nonprofit and independent.
